Within 7 days of purchase, yes. Email us and we will sort it.

recommendations.footerNote: Evidence-ranked · Updated 2025 · Click any card to deep dive

sleep.label: Better Sleep
sleep.intro: Good sleep is the foundation of every other health goal. Before stacking supplements, address the basics: consistent wake time, a dark cool room, and no screens 30 minutes before bed.

muscle.label: Build Muscle
muscle.intro: Supplements accelerate gains — but only on a foundation of adequate protein (1.6–2.2 g/kg), progressive overload, and 7–9 h sleep. Get those right first.

health.label: General Health
health.intro: A short, evidence-led list covers most nutritional gaps from a modern diet. Whole foods come first — but these fill in what diet alone commonly misses, especially at northern latitudes.

energy.label: Energy & Focus
energy.intro: Low energy is almost always a lifestyle problem first — poor sleep, chronic stress, or nutrient deficiency. Fix those root causes before adding stimulants.

cognition.label: Brain & Cognition
cognition.intro: Sleep is the number-one nootropic. Aerobic exercise (BDNF) and stress management come next. Supplements then optimize on top of that foundation.

ingredientQuality.multiIngredientFormula: Multi-ingredient formula · Select an ingredient to analyze

creatine-monohydrate.tagline: The most researched strength & power supplement on earth
creatine-monohydrate.primaryUse: Increases ATP regeneration for sustained power output and muscle mass gains
creatine-monohydrate.overview: Creatine is the best return-on-investment supplement for strength and muscle — bar none. Your muscles store it as phosphocreatine, which rapidly refuels ATP during explosive efforts like heavy sets and sprints. It also draws water into muscle cells, contributing to a fuller look and accelerating protein synthesis over time.
keyFacts: Backed by 500+ human clinical trials — the most studied supplement in sports science
Loading phase (20g/day for 5 days) is optional — daily 3g gives the same result in ~4 weeks
Safe for long-term use; no evidence of kidney harm in healthy individuals
commonMistakes: Doing a loading phase — it's optional, not required. Daily 3–5g reaches saturation in ~4 weeks without the GI issues a loading phase causes.
Stopping during a cut — creatine is equally valuable in a caloric deficit where it helps preserve strength and muscle mass.

caffeine.tagline: Proven ergogenic aid for endurance, strength, and focus
caffeine.primaryUse: Stimulates the CNS via adenosine receptor antagonism, reducing perceived exertion
caffeine.overview: Caffeine blocks adenosine — the chemical that accumulates in your brain and makes you feel tired. The result is reduced perceived effort: hard workouts feel easier. It reliably improves both endurance and strength performance, making it one of the most validated ergogenic aids in sports science.
keyFacts: Tolerance builds within 1–2 weeks; cycling off restores sensitivity
Anhydrous caffeine absorbs faster than coffee for more predictable timing
Half-life is ~5 hours — avoid doses within 6 hours of sleep
commonMistakes: Taking it too late — with a ~5-hour half-life, a 3pm dose can still be active at 11pm and significantly wreck sleep quality even if you fall asleep fine.
Staying on it continuously — tolerance builds fast. Cycling off 1–2 days per week (or one week per month) keeps sensitivity high and the effect potent.

beta-alanine.tagline: Buffer muscle acid to train harder for longer
beta-alanine.primaryUse: Raises muscle carnosine levels, delaying fatigue in 60–240 second efforts
beta-alanine.overview: Beta-alanine doesn't work in real time — it builds up carnosine in your muscles over weeks. Carnosine acts as a pH buffer, soaking up the acid that accumulates during intense efforts and delaying the burning sensation that forces you to stop. It's most effective for sustained efforts in the 1–4 minute range like rowing, cycling intervals, or combat sports rounds.
keyFacts: The "tingling" (paresthesia) is harmless and fades with regular use
Takes 4–6 weeks to meaningfully raise muscle carnosine levels
Split into 2×1.6g doses to reduce tingling intensity
commonMistakes: Expecting an acute pre-workout effect — beta-alanine requires 4–6 weeks of daily use to build meaningful carnosine levels; single doses do nothing.
Skipping it on non-training days — consistent daily use is what builds carnosine stores, not just pre-workout doses.

citrulline-malate.tagline: Nitric oxide precursor for pumps and endurance
citrulline-malate.primaryUse: Increases arginine bioavailability, enhancing NO production and reducing fatigue
citrulline-malate.overview: Citrulline converts to arginine in the kidneys, bypassing the liver degradation that makes arginine supplements ineffective. More arginine means more nitric oxide, which dilates blood vessels for better oxygen and nutrient delivery — the pump you feel in training. It also helps recycle ammonia, a fatigue byproduct, reducing next-day soreness.
keyFacts: More effective than L-Arginine directly — bypasses first-pass liver metabolism
2:1 malate ratio is the best-studied form; pure L-citrulline is also effective
Reduces muscle soreness ratings by ~40% in multiple studies
commonMistakes: Using L-Arginine instead — it's broken down by the liver before reaching circulation, making citrulline 2–3× more effective at raising nitric oxide levels.
Underdosing — most studies showing benefits used 6–8g, but many pre-workout blends contain only 2–3g of citrulline.

betaine-anhydrous.tagline: Osmolyte that supports power output and body composition
betaine-anhydrous.primaryUse: Acts as a methyl donor and osmolyte; supports creatine synthesis and hydration
betaine-anhydrous.overview: Betaine is found naturally in beets, spinach, and quinoa. Your body uses it as a methyl donor for dozens of metabolic processes, and in training it acts as an osmolyte — helping cells maintain hydration under stress. Results are subtle but compound over weeks, with body composition improvements most evident after 6+ weeks of consistent use.
keyFacts: Found naturally in beets and spinach — used in food processing for centuries
Methyl-donor function supports homocysteine metabolism and liver health
Consistent use over 6+ weeks shows body composition improvements
commonMistakes: Expecting immediate effects like caffeine — betaine works through slow metabolic processes; body composition shifts take 6+ weeks to appear.
Taking it all at once — splitting doses (morning + pre-workout) improves cell saturation and tolerability over a single large dose.

hmb.primaryUse: Reduces muscle protein breakdown, especially useful during caloric restriction
hmb.overview: HMB is what your body naturally makes from leucine — the key muscle-building amino acid. It acts as a brake on muscle breakdown, which is especially valuable when calories are restricted or when returning from a training break. Its benefits are most clearly seen in older athletes and during high-stress periods where muscle loss is the primary concern.
keyFacts: A metabolite of leucine — body produces ~0.2–0.4g naturally from dietary protein
Most benefit seen in untrained individuals or during periods of high muscle stress
Free-acid form (HMB-FA) absorbs faster than calcium HMB
commonMistakes: Using it as a primary muscle-builder — evidence for HMB in well-trained athletes on adequate protein is weak. It shines most during caloric deficits or detraining.
Using calcium HMB instead of the free-acid form — HMB-FA absorbs significantly faster and has better acute performance data.

sodium-bicarbonate.tagline: Old-school acid buffer for high-intensity efforts
sodium-bicarbonate.primaryUse: Raises extracellular pH, buffering acid and improving performance in 1–7 minute efforts
sodium-bicarbonate.overview: Plain baking soda works as a performance aid because it raises blood pH, giving your muscles more buffer capacity to clear acid during intense efforts. It has been used by competitive athletes since the 1930s and has solid evidence for events lasting 1–7 minutes — from 800m track to MMA rounds. The main challenge is tolerating it without GI distress.
keyFacts: One of the original sports supplements — used by athletes since the 1930s
Take 60–90 min before exercise; GI distress is the main side effect (use enteric capsules)
Loading over 3–5 days can reduce GI issues and maintain performance
commonMistakes: Taking it on an empty stomach — GI distress (nausea, cramps) is the main barrier; always take with a light meal and plenty of water.
Taking it too close to the event — allow 60–90 minutes for absorption, and consider a 3–5 day lead-in dose to adapt your gut before competition.

taurine.tagline: Essential amino acid for hydration, endurance, and heart function
taurine.primaryUse: Acts as an osmolyte regulating cell volume under stress; supports mitochondrial function and reduces exercise-induced oxidative damage
taurine.overview: Taurine is one of the most abundant amino acids in muscle tissue and plays a structural role in keeping cells hydrated during exercise. It acts as an osmolyte — drawing water into cells to maintain volume under stress — and reduces exercise-induced oxidative damage. It is the reason taurine is a mainstay in every major energy drink formula, though its effect is not stimulant-based. Unlike caffeine, taurine is calming and neuroprotective, counterbalancing the overstimulation caffeine can produce.
keyFacts: Second most abundant amino acid in muscle tissue after glutamine
Found in energy drinks not for stimulation but for cellular hydration and fatigue protection — the caffeine provides the stimulation
Conditionally essential during intense training — demand can exceed endogenous synthesis capacity
commonMistakes: Thinking taurine is a stimulant because it's in energy drinks — it's actually calming and neuroprotective; the stimulation comes from caffeine. They serve complementary, not overlapping, roles.
Underdosing — most energy drinks contain only 500–1000mg. Performance benefits are better supported at 1–3g per day.

magnesium-glycinate.tagline: The premium relaxation mineral — gentle and highly absorbed
magnesium-glycinate.primaryUse: Activates GABA receptors and supports the parasympathetic nervous system
magnesium-glycinate.overview: Magnesium is involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions, including directly activating GABA — your brain's main calming neurotransmitter. Most people don't get enough from diet alone, and deficiency commonly shows up as restlessness, muscle cramps, and fragmented sleep. Glycinate pairs magnesium with the amino acid glycine, which itself has independent sleep-promoting properties.
keyFacts: Glycinate chelation makes it one of the most bioavailable forms with minimal laxative effect
Up to 68% of the population is estimated to be deficient in magnesium
Takes 4–8 weeks of consistent use to normalize deficiency symptoms
commonMistakes: Using magnesium oxide for sleep — it has <10% absorption and a strong laxative effect, providing little actual calming benefit.
Expecting instant results — correcting a deficiency takes 4–8 weeks of nightly use; don't judge it in the first week.

melatonin.tagline: Regulate your circadian clock with precision
melatonin.primaryUse: Signals sleep onset by binding MT1/MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus
melatonin.overview: Melatonin is a hormone your brain naturally produces when it gets dark — it signals your body that it's time to sleep. Supplementing doesn't knock you out like a sedative; instead it shifts your internal clock, making it most valuable for jet lag, shift work, or delayed sleep phase. Both timing and dose matter far more than most people realize.
keyFacts: More is not better — 0.5mg is as effective as 5mg for sleep onset in most adults
Works best for circadian phase-shifting, not as a sedative
Light exposure (especially blue light) suppresses natural melatonin production by 50%+
commonMistakes: Taking too high a dose — 0.5mg is as effective as 5mg for most people and avoids next-day grogginess. Higher doses are not stronger, just longer-lasting.
Using it as a nightly sedative for chronic sleep issues — melatonin is a circadian regulator. For ongoing sleep problems, address sleep hygiene and light exposure first.

l-theanine.primaryUse: Increases alpha brain wave activity, promoting relaxed alertness and sleep onset
l-theanine.overview: L-Theanine is an amino acid unique to tea leaves that promotes alpha brain waves — the mental state associated with calm, unfocused awareness. It's non-sedating, so it won't make you drowsy during the day, but it quiets the mental chatter that can prevent sleep onset at night. It also pairs exceptionally well with caffeine to smooth out jitteriness.
bestFor: Stacking with caffeine for calm focus
keyFacts: Naturally found in green tea — typically 20–30mg per cup
Does not cause grogginess; alpha wave effect is non-sedating
200mg + 100mg caffeine is one of the most studied cognitive stacks
commonMistakes: Underdosing — green tea contains only 20–30mg per cup. Therapeutic effects for sleep or focus require 100–200mg from a supplement.
Expecting a strong sedative effect — it's subtle and calming, not a knockout. Those with significant anxiety or insomnia may need to stack it with other approaches.

ashwagandha.tagline: Adaptogen that lowers cortisol and improves sleep quality
ashwagandha.primaryUse: Reduces cortisol and stress biomarkers, improving non-REM sleep duration
ashwagandha.overview: Ashwagandha is one of the most well-researched adaptogens — plants that help your body modulate its stress response. It directly lowers cortisol, the hormone that keeps your nervous system in alert mode and fragments deep sleep. The KSM-66 extract specifically uses root-only material and has the strongest clinical validation among ashwagandha forms.
keyFacts: KSM-66 is a root-only extract with 5% withanolides — the most clinically tested form
Cortisol reduction of ~28% reported in 8-week RCT vs placebo
Dual benefit: improves sleep quality AND may modestly boost testosterone
commonMistakes: Using cheap root powder instead of a standardized extract — active withanolide content varies wildly in unverified powders; KSM-66 or Sensoril with stated percentages are essential.
Expecting fast results — cortisol normalization is a slow process. Give it 4–8 weeks of consistent daily use before evaluating.

glycine.tagline: Amino acid that lowers core body temperature for better sleep
glycine.primaryUse: Facilitates sleep by reducing core body temperature and modulating NMDA receptors
glycine.overview: Glycine is a simple amino acid with a surprisingly direct effect on sleep: it lowers your core body temperature by dilating blood vessels in the skin, and cooling is one of the body's key triggers for sleep onset. Users consistently report waking up feeling less fatigued the next morning — a sign of improved sleep architecture rather than just longer time in bed.
bestFor: Poor sleep quality without clear cause
keyFacts: Lowers core body temperature by ~0.3°C — a known trigger for sleep onset
Subjective reports show significantly reduced daytime fatigue the next morning
Extremely safe at any dose — also used at 15–60g daily for metabolic conditions
commonMistakes: Overlooking it because it's "just an amino acid" — the core temperature-lowering mechanism is distinct from other sleep aids and complements magnesium well.
Taking it too early — the temperature drop needs to coincide with your sleep window; take it 30–60 minutes before you actually intend to sleep.

5-htp.tagline: Serotonin precursor for mood, sleep onset, and appetite control
5-htp.primaryUse: Directly converts to serotonin in the brain, which is subsequently converted to melatonin in the pineal gland
5-htp.overview: 5-HTP is the immediate precursor to serotonin and crosses the blood-brain barrier more efficiently than tryptophan, which competes with other amino acids for transport. The result is a more reliable increase in serotonin and downstream melatonin, making it useful for sleep onset, mood stabilization, and appetite regulation. Its dual serotonin/melatonin action makes it especially effective for people whose sleep issues are driven by anxiety or low mood.
keyFacts: More efficient at raising brain serotonin than L-tryptophan — bypasses the rate-limiting conversion step
Do not combine with SSRIs or MAOIs without medical supervision — serotonin syndrome risk
Sourced from Griffonia simplicifolia seed extract — no animal-derived ingredients
commonMistakes: Combining with SSRIs or MAOIs — 5-HTP increases serotonin activity, directly overlapping with antidepressant mechanisms; this combination can cause serotonin syndrome, a potentially serious condition.
Taking it indefinitely without cycling — prolonged continuous use can downregulate serotonin receptors; cycle off every 4–6 weeks or use on an as-needed basis.

valerian-root.tagline: Traditional sleep herb with GABA-modulating properties
valerian-root.primaryUse: Increases GABA availability by inhibiting its breakdown and reuptake, reducing neurological excitation
valerian-root.overview: Valerian root is one of the oldest sleep remedies in Western herbalism, used since ancient Greece and Rome. Modern research suggests it works by increasing GABA — the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter — by inhibiting its breakdown and reuptake. Unlike pharmaceutical GABA modulators, it achieves this gently without significant sedative hangover in most users. Effects are often more pronounced after 2–4 weeks of consistent use than on the first night.
keyFacts: Valerenic acid is the primary active compound — look for standardized extracts specifying valerenic acid content
Evidence is stronger for consistent 2–4 week use than for single-dose acute effects
Pairs well with lemon balm (Melissa officinalis) — several clinical trials use this combination
commonMistakes: Judging it on the first night — acute single-dose effects are mild; the strongest clinical evidence is for regular use over 2–4 weeks for sleep latency improvement.
Assuming more is better — doses above 900mg can have a paradoxical stimulating effect in some individuals; stick to 300–600mg.

lions-mane.tagline: The nerve growth factor mushroom for brain health
lions-mane.primaryUse: Stimulates NGF synthesis, supporting neuroplasticity and long-term cognitive health
lions-mane.overview: Lion's Mane is one of the few supplements with evidence for stimulating Nerve Growth Factor (NGF) — a protein essential for the growth and maintenance of brain neurons. It's not a stimulant; effects are slow-building and cumulative over months, supporting cognitive health rather than delivering acute focus. Most valuable for long-term brain maintenance and anxiety-related focus issues.
keyFacts: Hericenones and erinacines are the active compounds that cross the blood-brain barrier
Fruiting body extracts are more potent than mycelium-only products
Human trials show promise for mild cognitive impairment; more data needed
commonMistakes: Buying mycelium-on-grain products — these contain mostly starch filler, not active compounds. Look for fruiting body extracts standardized to beta-glucan content.
Expecting an immediate nootropic effect — benefits accumulate over 8+ weeks and are about long-term brain health, not acute focus or energy.

bacopa-monnieri.tagline: Ayurvedic herb for long-term memory consolidation
bacopa-monnieri.primaryUse: Enhances memory formation via antioxidant activity and cholinergic modulation
bacopa-monnieri.overview: Bacopa is one of the best-studied herbs for memory, used in Ayurvedic medicine for centuries and validated in modern RCTs. It enhances synaptic communication and protects neurons from oxidative stress, translating to faster information recall and better learning retention over time. It won't make you sharper tomorrow — but consistently over 8–12 weeks, the evidence is compelling.
keyFacts: Effects are slow-building — most trials show significant results at 8–12 weeks
Bacosides A and B are the active components — look for 20–55% standardization
Best taken with a fat-containing meal for improved absorption
commonMistakes: Giving up too early — most trials show meaningful cognitive improvement only at 8–12 weeks; quitting after 2–3 weeks means missing the benefit entirely.
Taking on an empty stomach — bacosides are fat-soluble; absorption is significantly improved when taken with a meal containing fat.

alpha-gpc.tagline: Premium choline source for acetylcholine synthesis
alpha-gpc.primaryUse: Crosses the blood-brain barrier to rapidly boost acetylcholine levels
alpha-gpc.overview: Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter behind focus, learning, and the mind-muscle connection in training. Alpha-GPC is the most efficient way to raise brain acetylcholine levels because it crosses the blood-brain barrier directly, unlike most choline sources. Athletes use it for cognitive sharpness and its reported boost to power output and growth hormone release.
keyFacts: Contains ~40% choline by weight — one of the most bioavailable choline forms
Also has independent nootropic effects beyond just choline donation
A single 600mg dose may acutely increase growth hormone release
commonMistakes: Substituting CDP-choline without adjusting dose — CDP-choline contains ~18% choline vs Alpha-GPC's ~40%; you'd need roughly double the milligrams.
Stacking with racetams without enough choline — racetams increase acetylcholine demand; inadequate choline supply leads to brain fog and headaches.

rhodiola-rosea.tagline: Adaptogen that fights mental fatigue under stress
rhodiola-rosea.primaryUse: Modulates stress hormones and supports serotonergic/dopaminergic transmission
rhodiola-rosea.overview: Rhodiola has one key advantage over most adaptogens: it works acutely. You can feel anti-fatigue and mood-stabilizing effects within hours of a dose, not just after weeks of use. It modulates the stress response axis and supports dopaminergic and serotonergic transmission — genuinely useful during exam periods, high-deadline weeks, or hard training blocks.
keyFacts: Acts within hours for acute anti-fatigue effects — unlike most adaptogens
Cyclic use (5 days on, 2 days off) may prevent tolerance buildup
Used by Soviet military and athletes for decades before Western research began
commonMistakes: Using it continuously without breaks — tolerance appears to build; cycling 5 days on / 2 days off (or taking weekends off) preserves effectiveness over time.
Taking it late in the day — it's mildly activating and can interfere with sleep if taken in the afternoon or evening.

panax-ginseng.tagline: Classic adaptogen for energy and cognitive resilience
panax-ginseng.primaryUse: Ginsenosides modulate the HPA axis and multiple neurotransmitter systems
panax-ginseng.overview: Panax ginseng is one of the most thoroughly researched adaptogens, with evidence spanning cognitive performance, immune function, and blood sugar regulation. Its active ginsenosides modulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis — essentially the body's stress-response control center. Korean Red Ginseng, made by steaming the root, has the strongest clinical track record of any form.
keyFacts: Korean Red Ginseng (steamed & dried) has the most clinical evidence
Ginsenoside Rg1 and Rb1 are primary active compounds
May modestly improve blood glucose regulation when used consistently
commonMistakes: Confusing it with Siberian (Eleuthero) or American Ginseng — different plants with different active compounds and use cases; not interchangeable.
Using unstandardized products — active ginsenoside content varies enormously between brands. Always look for a product specifying total ginsenoside percentage.

phosphatidylserine.tagline: Cell membrane phospholipid essential for brain function
phosphatidylserine.primaryUse: Supports neuronal membrane fluidity and blunts the cortisol response to stress
phosphatidylserine.overview: Phosphatidylserine is a phospholipid that forms a critical component of every brain cell membrane. As we age, PS levels decline, affecting memory and processing speed. It also blunts the cortisol spike after intense exercise — making it uniquely valuable for athletes who want to recover faster without suppressing the training adaptations that cortisol also drives.
keyFacts: FDA allows a qualified health claim for PS and cognitive decline risk reduction
Soy-derived PS (most common) has equivalent bioavailability to sunflower-derived
Cortisol-blunting effect is most pronounced at 400–800mg — relevant post-exercise
commonMistakes: Skipping it due to cost — lower doses of 100–200mg still provide meaningful cortisol-blunting effects for athletes at a more manageable price point.
Taking on an empty stomach — PS is fat-soluble and absorption is significantly improved when taken with a fat-containing meal.

alcar.tagline: Mitochondrial fuel transporter with a nootropic edge
alcar.primaryUse: Shuttles fatty acids into mitochondria for energy; the acetyl group crosses the blood-brain barrier to support acetylcholine synthesis
alcar.overview: ALCAR does two things simultaneously: the carnitine moiety shuttles long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria to be burned for energy, and the acetyl group crosses the blood-brain barrier and contributes to acetylcholine synthesis — the same neurotransmitter that Alpha-GPC targets. This dual action makes it a rare crossover between a metabolic supplement and a nootropic. Evidence is strongest for age-related cognitive decline, neuropathic pain, and fatigue, with modest but real benefits for focus and energy in healthy adults.
bestFor: Older adults with age-related energy and cognitive decline
keyFacts: Only the acetylated form (ALCAR) — not plain L-Carnitine — crosses the blood-brain barrier for cognitive effects
Best-documented clinical use is neuropathic pain and age-related cognitive decline
Carnitine biosynthesis declines with age; dietary red meat is the primary food source
commonMistakes: Using plain L-Carnitine for nootropic effects — only the acetylated ALCAR form crosses the blood-brain barrier; plain carnitine stays in the periphery and has no central nervous system activity.
Expecting dramatic fat loss — the metabolic benefit is real but modest; ALCAR enhances fat as a fuel source during exercise, not as a standalone thermogenic.

citicoline.tagline: Premium choline source that doubles as a brain membrane builder
citicoline.primaryUse: Donates choline for acetylcholine synthesis while the cytidine component converts to uridine, supporting neuronal membrane phospholipid synthesis
citicoline.overview: Citicoline is a two-in-one nootropic: it provides choline for acetylcholine synthesis (like Alpha-GPC) while its cytidine component converts to uridine — a compound that supports neuronal membrane repair and dopaminergic activity. This means citicoline not only boosts acetylcholine but also supports the structural maintenance of neurons and may improve mood via dopamine pathways. It is approved as a pharmaceutical drug for stroke recovery and cognitive impairment across Europe and Japan.
keyFacts: Provides ~18% choline by weight vs Alpha-GPC's ~40% — but the uridine component adds independent neuroplasticity benefits Alpha-GPC does not offer
Approved as a pharmaceutical drug for stroke and cognitive disorders in Europe and Japan
May increase dopamine receptor density with long-term use — relevant for motivation and mood
commonMistakes: Directly substituting for Alpha-GPC at the same dose — citicoline contains ~18% choline vs Alpha-GPC's ~40%; you'd need roughly double the milligrams for equivalent choline output, though the uridine benefit partially offsets this.
Expecting an acute stimulant-like effect — cognitive improvements from citicoline are subtle and cumulative over weeks, not immediate sharpness.

ginkgo-biloba.tagline: Ancient tree extract for cerebral circulation and cognitive longevity
ginkgo-biloba.primaryUse: Increases cerebral blood flow via platelet-activating factor inhibition and provides neuroprotective antioxidant activity
ginkgo-biloba.overview: Ginkgo biloba is one of the oldest living tree species and one of the most widely purchased supplements globally. Its leaves contain flavonoid glycosides and ginkgolides that increase cerebral blood flow and protect neurons from oxidative damage. Evidence is strongest for age-related cognitive decline, where improved circulation produces meaningful functional differences. In healthy younger adults, improvements in attention speed and processing are the most reproducible finding.
bestFor: Tinnitus and vertigo from circulation issues
keyFacts: EGb 761® is the most clinically studied extract — standardized to 24% ginkgo flavone glycosides and 6% terpene lactones
Inhibits platelet-activating factor — a mild blood-thinning effect; caution with anticoagulants
One of the most studied natural compounds for dementia prevention in European geriatric medicine
commonMistakes: Using unstandardized ginkgo products — active compound concentration varies enormously between brands; always look for EGb 761® or explicit 24% flavonoids / 6% terpenes on the label.
Taking it alongside anticoagulants without medical advice — ginkgo's platelet-inhibiting effect meaningfully increases bleeding risk when combined with warfarin or daily aspirin.

tart-cherry.tagline: Natural anti-inflammatory for post-exercise muscle soreness
tart-cherry.primaryUse: Anthocyanins reduce oxidative stress and muscle damage markers post-exercise
tart-cherry.overview: Tart cherries are exceptionally rich in anthocyanins — the pigments behind their deep red color that act as potent antioxidants. After hard training, your muscles undergo significant oxidative stress; these anthocyanins accelerate the cleanup process, reducing soreness and speeding up strength recovery. As a bonus, tart cherries also contain natural melatonin for a sleep quality benefit.
keyFacts: Also improves sleep quality due to natural melatonin content
Most effective when started 4–5 days before a high-stress event
Montmorency variety has the highest anthocyanin concentration
commonMistakes: Starting it the morning of an event — most of the evidence uses a 4–7 day loading period before the high-stress activity to saturate tissues with anthocyanins.
Using sweet cherry juice instead of tart Montmorency — the anthocyanin concentration in sweet varieties is dramatically lower and likely insufficient for the effect.

omega-3.tagline: Essential fatty acids for systemic inflammation control
omega-3.primaryUse: EPA/DHA reduce prostaglandin-mediated inflammation and support joint health
omega-3.overview: EPA and DHA are long-chain fatty acids your body cannot efficiently synthesize — they must come from diet or supplements. EPA is the primary driver of anti-inflammatory activity, directly competing with the inflammatory pathways behind joint pain and post-exercise soreness. DHA concentrates in brain cell membranes and supports cognitive function. Most Western diets are severely deficient in both.
keyFacts: EPA primarily drives anti-inflammatory effects; DHA supports brain structure
Triglyceride form (fish oil) absorbs ~70% better than ethyl ester form
Look for IFOS or similar 3rd-party certification for purity and oxidation levels
commonMistakes: Counting total fish oil milligrams instead of EPA+DHA — a "1000mg fish oil" capsule typically contains only 300mg of actual omega-3s. Check the label.
Using rancid oil — oxidized fish oil is pro-inflammatory. Check for a fresh smell (not fishy), look for IFOS certification, and keep it refrigerated after opening.

collagen-peptides.tagline: Structural protein for tendons, joints, and connective tissue
collagen-peptides.primaryUse: Provides hydroxyproline and glycine substrates for connective tissue collagen synthesis
collagen-peptides.overview: Tendons, ligaments, and cartilage are made of collagen but have poor blood supply, making connective tissue injuries notoriously slow to heal. Taking hydrolyzed collagen 30–60 minutes before exercise has been shown to increase collagen synthesis specifically in tendons during the post-exercise window. Vitamin C is a required co-factor — without it, the synthesis process is incomplete.
keyFacts: Take 30–60 min before exercise for maximum collagen synthesis stimulation in connective tissue
Vitamin C is a required co-factor for collagen synthesis — combine them
Hydrolyzed form (peptides) has significantly better absorption than gelatin
commonMistakes: Taking it without vitamin C — collagen synthesis requires vitamin C as a co-factor. Skipping it means significantly reduced connective tissue benefit.
Using collagen as a general protein supplement — its amino acid profile lacks tryptophan and is not optimal for muscle building; its purpose is specifically connective tissue.

curcumin.tagline: Potent anti-inflammatory compound from turmeric root
curcumin.primaryUse: Inhibits NF-kB pathway, reducing systemic inflammatory cytokines
curcumin.overview: Curcumin is the active compound in turmeric and works by blocking NF-kB — one of the master switches of inflammation in the body. The critical catch is bioavailability: standard curcumin is almost completely insoluble and barely absorbed. With piperine from black pepper, absorption jumps by up to 2000%. Without an absorption enhancer, you're essentially taking an inert powder.
keyFacts: Standard curcumin has <1% bioavailability alone — piperine boosts absorption by ~2000%
Newer forms like Meriva® (phytosome), BCM-95® and Longvida® absorb even better
Curcumin is only ~3% of dried turmeric by weight — supplements far exceed food amounts
commonMistakes: Relying on turmeric in food for a therapeutic effect — you'd need to eat enormous quantities, and the fat and piperine required for absorption still aren't guaranteed.
Buying standard curcumin without an absorption enhancer — piperine, Meriva®, or BCM-95® are not optional; without them, bioavailability is below 1%.

glutamine.tagline: Conditionally essential amino acid for gut and immune health
glutamine.primaryUse: Most abundant amino acid; supports gut barrier integrity and immune cell function
glutamine.overview: Glutamine is the most abundant amino acid in muscle tissue and the primary fuel source for gut lining cells and immune lymphocytes. During intense training, illness, or injury, your body's glutamine demand can exceed its production capacity — leading to muscle catabolism and immune suppression. Supplementing helps plug this gap, with the strongest evidence for gut barrier integrity.
glutamine.typicalDose: 5–10g daily (or up to 20g in clinical settings)
keyFacts: Becomes "conditionally essential" during high physiological stress or injury
Primary fuel source for enterocytes (gut lining cells) and immune lymphocytes
High-dose evidence for performance is weak; gut/immune benefits are better supported
commonMistakes: Expecting muscle-building effects comparable to creatine — evidence for glutamine improving hypertrophy or strength in well-nourished athletes is weak.
Overlooking the gut health application — the strongest case for glutamine supplementation is gut barrier integrity ("leaky gut"), not muscle performance.

msm.tagline: Bioavailable sulfur for joints, cartilage, and post-exercise inflammation
msm.primaryUse: Provides organic sulfur for collagen, cartilage, and glutathione synthesis; reduces inflammatory cytokines post-exercise
msm.overview: MSM is an organic sulfur compound found naturally in plants, meat, and dairy — though cooking destroys most of it. Sulfur is a structural component of collagen, cartilage, keratin, and glutathione, making MSM a useful substrate for connective tissue repair and antioxidant defense. Clinical evidence shows it reduces joint pain and stiffness in osteoarthritis and decreases exercise-induced muscle damage markers. It is most commonly stacked with collagen peptides and glucosamine, where combined evidence is stronger than any single agent alone.
keyFacts: Sulfur from MSM is incorporated into collagen and cartilage within 24 hours — highly bioavailable
Synergistic with glucosamine and chondroitin — most joint formulas combine all three
Also reduces allergy symptoms by inhibiting IgE-mediated mast cell histamine release
commonMistakes: Expecting rapid relief like an NSAID — MSM works by supporting tissue structure over weeks, not by acutely blocking pain; give it 4–6 weeks before evaluating effectiveness.
Taking a single large daily dose — splitting into two doses (morning and evening) improves tolerability and provides more consistent tissue sulfur availability throughout the day.

vitamin-d3-k2.tagline: The essential sunshine vitamin pair for bone and immune health
vitamin-d3-k2.primaryUse: D3 regulates calcium absorption; K2 directs calcium to bones rather than arteries
vitamin-d3-k2.overview: Vitamin D acts more like a hormone than a vitamin — it regulates gene expression across hundreds of tissues and is essential for immune function, bone density, and testosterone production. Most people in northern latitudes or who work indoors are deficient. K2 is the critical partner: D3 increases calcium absorption, and K2 ensures that calcium goes to your bones rather than calcifying your arteries.
keyFacts: Estimated 40–50% of adults worldwide are deficient; deficiency linked to 200+ conditions
K2 prevents the "calcium paradox" — D3 increases calcium absorption, K2 ensures it goes to bone not arteries
Cholecalciferol (D3) raises blood levels ~3× more effectively than ergocalciferol (D2)
commonMistakes: Taking D3 long-term without K2 — without K2, increased calcium absorption from D3 can deposit in arteries instead of bones. The two work as a pair.
Stopping supplementation once levels look "normal" — standard lab reference ranges are often set too low. Optimal function is typically at 40–60 ng/mL, not just above deficiency threshold.

zinc-bisglycinate.tagline: Essential mineral for immunity, hormones, and wound healing
zinc-bisglycinate.primaryUse: Cofactor in 300+ enzymatic reactions; critical for testosterone synthesis and immune function
zinc-bisglycinate.overview: Zinc is a cofactor in over 300 enzymes and plays a direct role in testosterone synthesis, immune activation, and wound repair. Athletes are particularly at risk for depletion since zinc is lost in sweat and high training volumes increase metabolic demand. The bisglycinate chelate form absorbs dramatically more effectively than the zinc oxide found in cheap multivitamins.
keyFacts: Bisglycinate chelate absorbs ~43% more efficiently than zinc oxide
Zinc competes with copper — long-term high doses (>40mg) may need copper balancing
Deficiency is associated with reduced testosterone, impaired wound healing, and increased illness
commonMistakes: Relying on zinc oxide from cheap multivitamins — it has <10% absorption. Bisglycinate or picolinate forms are significantly more effective per milligram.
Taking high doses long-term without balancing copper — zinc and copper compete for absorption; sustained doses above 40mg/day can gradually deplete copper.

magnesium-malate.tagline: Daytime magnesium form that supports energy metabolism
magnesium-malate.primaryUse: Malate participates in the Krebs cycle; supports ATP production and energy output
magnesium-malate.overview: Magnesium malate is the daytime-optimized magnesium form, pairing the mineral with malic acid — a key intermediate in the Krebs cycle that your cells use to produce ATP. Unlike magnesium glycinate (which has a calming, sleep-oriented effect), malate supports cellular energy without the sedating component, making it better suited for morning or pre-workout use.
keyFacts: Malic acid is a key Krebs cycle intermediate — this form supports cellular energy, not just relaxation
Less sedating than glycinate — better taken in the morning or pre-workout
Used in fibromyalgia research where malic acid + magnesium showed pain reduction
commonMistakes: Using magnesium glycinate in the morning and feeling sluggish — glycinate's calming effect is better suited for evening. Malate is the right form for daytime energy.
Confusing total compound weight with elemental magnesium content — the label's milligrams refer to the full compound, not just the active magnesium; check the elemental amount.

probiotics.tagline: Gut microbiome support for digestion and immunity
probiotics.primaryUse: Restore healthy gut flora balance and modulate the gut-immune axis
probiotics.overview: Your gut microbiome influences far more than digestion — it modulates immune function, mental health via the gut-brain axis, and systemic inflammation. Probiotics introduce beneficial bacteria to compete with harmful strains and support the gut lining. Multi-strain formulas outperform single-strain in most studies because different strains colonize different regions of the gut.
keyFacts: Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium genera have the most clinical evidence
Multi-strain formulas show better outcomes than single-strain in most studies
Refrigerated probiotics maintain higher viability — check the expiry and storage
commonMistakes: Leaving refrigerated probiotics at room temperature — viability degrades quickly without cold storage; always check storage requirements and expiry date before buying.
Starting at a high CFU dose immediately — some people experience significant gas and bloating. Start at 5–10 billion CFU and increase gradually over 1–2 weeks.

vitamin-c.tagline: Foundational antioxidant and immune system pillar
vitamin-c.primaryUse: Cofactor in collagen synthesis; reduces oxidative stress and supports immune function
vitamin-c.overview: Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for collagen synthesis and a frontline antioxidant that neutralizes free radicals generated during exercise and illness. Your body can't store large amounts, so consistent daily intake matters more than occasional megadosing. It's also essential to pair with collagen peptides — without adequate vitamin C, collagen synthesis is incomplete regardless of dose.
keyFacts: Absorption saturates at ~400mg per dose — split into 2x for better uptake
Does not prevent colds but reduces duration by ~8% in adults (14% in athletes)
Buffered forms (sodium ascorbate) are gentler on the stomach at high doses
commonMistakes: Megadosing in one go — absorption plateaus at ~400mg per dose; a single 1000mg dose wastes most of it in urine. Split into two smaller doses instead.
Only taking it when sick — vitamin C doesn't prevent colds but reduces duration when levels are already maintained through daily intake.

berberine.tagline: Plant alkaloid with significant metabolic effects
berberine.primaryUse: Activates AMPK, improving insulin sensitivity and blood glucose regulation
berberine.overview: Berberine is a plant alkaloid that activates AMPK — often called the "metabolic master switch" — leading to improved insulin sensitivity and lower blood glucose. It has a remarkably consistent track record in clinical trials, with multiple meta-analyses comparing its blood sugar effects favorably to Metformin. It's one of the most evidence-backed natural compounds for metabolic health.
keyFacts: Multiple meta-analyses show blood glucose reductions comparable to Metformin
Poor bioavailability — dihydroberberine (DHB) absorbs ~5× better
Not recommended during pregnancy; may interact with CYP enzymes
commonMistakes: Using standard berberine and being disappointed by weak effects — its bioavailability is poor. Dihydroberberine (DHB) absorbs ~5× more efficiently.
Taking it away from meals — berberine's glucose-lowering effect is most relevant around mealtimes; take 20–30 minutes before eating for best results.

coq10.tagline: Mitochondrial electron carrier for cellular energy and heart health
coq10.primaryUse: Transfers electrons in the mitochondrial electron transport chain for ATP production
coq10.overview: CoQ10 is a fat-soluble compound that acts as an electron shuttle in your mitochondria's energy production chain. Your body synthesizes it naturally, but levels peak around age 20 and decline steadily after 40. Statins dramatically accelerate this depletion by blocking the same metabolic pathway. Ubiquinol is the active, reduced form that bypasses a conversion step that becomes less efficient with age.
keyFacts: Ubiquinol (reduced form) absorbs up to 8× better than ubiquinone for adults over 40
Statin medications inhibit the same pathway that produces CoQ10 — supplementation is widely recommended
Body levels naturally decline with age, starting from around age 40
commonMistakes: Using ubiquinone (standard CoQ10) over the age of 40 — the ability to convert ubiquinone to active ubiquinol declines with age; ubiquinol absorbs directly and is worth the premium.
Taking it without food — CoQ10 is fat-soluble and absorption is dramatically reduced when taken on an empty stomach.

nac.tagline: Master antioxidant precursor and respiratory health cornerstone
nac.primaryUse: Replenishes intracellular glutathione — the body's primary endogenous antioxidant defense system
nac.overview: NAC is the rate-limiting precursor to glutathione — your body's most important antioxidant, present in virtually every cell. Unlike glutathione itself, NAC is absorbed efficiently and converted intracellularly on demand. It has a remarkably broad evidence base: it is a licensed pharmaceutical antidote for acetaminophen overdose, used clinically for COPD and mucus clearance, and has accumulating evidence for psychiatric conditions including OCD. Athletes use it to reduce exercise-induced oxidative stress, though timing around workouts requires nuance.
keyFacts: Licensed pharmaceutical antidote at high doses for acetaminophen (paracetamol) overdose — one of emergency medicine's most established uses
May reduce alcohol cravings — one of the more replicated findings in addiction research
Chronic use immediately around training sessions may blunt adaptation signals — some oxidative stress is required for training response
commonMistakes: Taking it pre- and post-workout every session — some reactive oxygen species are required for training adaptation signaling; chronic antioxidant blunting around workouts may reduce long-term gains.
Combining with nitroglycerin or other vasodilating medications — NAC potentiates vasodilation and can cause rapid blood pressure drops when paired with these drugs.

spirulina.tagline: Nutrient-dense algae with anti-inflammatory and iron-rich properties
spirulina.primaryUse: Dense source of complete protein, phycocyanin, iron, and B vitamins with broad antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity
spirulina.overview: Spirulina is a blue-green algae with extraordinary nutrient density: up to 70% protein by dry weight with a complete amino acid profile, iron, B vitamins, and phycocyanin — the blue pigment that drives most of its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. It is particularly relevant for plant-based athletes filling micronutrient gaps, for anyone with suboptimal iron status, and as a general anti-inflammatory stack addition. Quality and sourcing matter significantly — heavy metal contamination is a real concern with low-grade products.
spirulina.typicalDose: 1–3g daily (up to 8–10g in more therapeutic protocols)
bestFor: Plant-based athletes needing protein and iron
keyFacts: Up to 70% protein by dry weight with a complete amino acid profile — one of the most protein-dense whole foods
Phycocyanin, the blue-green pigment, is the primary anti-inflammatory active compound
Rich in iron and B vitamins — particularly valuable for vegan and vegetarian athletes
commonMistakes: Relying on spirulina as a meaningful protein source — the percentage is high but total grams per dose are low; you would need very large daily amounts to replace dietary protein.
Buying the cheapest available without sourcing transparency — spirulina absorbs heavy metals from contaminated water; always buy from brands that publish third-party heavy metal testing.

nmn.tagline: NAD+ precursor at the frontier of longevity science
nmn.primaryUse: Converts directly to NAD+ — a coenzyme essential for energy metabolism, DNA repair, and sirtuin longevity pathway activation
nmn.overview: NAD+ is a coenzyme that declines approximately 50% between ages 20 and 60, a decline now understood to be a central mechanism of aging — impairing mitochondrial energy production, DNA repair, and sirtuin activity. NMN converts directly to NAD+ in cells, making it one of the most efficient supplemental routes to restore levels. Human trials now show measurable NAD+ increases and improvements in muscle insulin sensitivity and arterial flexibility. The longevity narrative is somewhat ahead of the clinical evidence, but the mechanistic case is solid.
bestFor: Pairing with resveratrol in longevity stacks
keyFacts: NAD+ declines ~50% between age 20 and 60 — NMN supplementation demonstrably raises levels in human trials
Pairs synergistically with resveratrol — NMN raises NAD+, which is the required cofactor for SIRT1 that resveratrol activates
Sublingual forms may provide better bioavailability than standard oral capsules
commonMistakes: Choosing NMN based on price alone — purity and stability vary dramatically between brands; prioritize products with third-party purity testing or sublingual forms with published stability data.

Expecting dramatic short-term anti-aging effects — current human data shows meaningful biochemical changes but the longevity narrative outpaces clinical evidence; it is a promising piece of a broader protocol, not a standalone solution.

quercetin.tagline: Flavonoid powerhouse for immunity, inflammation, and allergies
quercetin.primaryUse: Inhibits histamine release from mast cells, suppresses NF-kB inflammatory signaling, and acts as a broad-spectrum antioxidant
quercetin.overview: Quercetin is one of the most abundant dietary flavonoids — found in onions, apples, and capers — but at concentrations far too low in food to produce clinical effects. As a supplement it inhibits histamine release (making it relevant for allergies and rhinitis), suppresses the NF-kB inflammatory pathway, and has antiviral properties under active investigation. Its bioavailability is naturally low, but pairing with Vitamin C or using phytosome delivery forms substantially extends its activity.
quercetin.typicalDose: 500–1000mg daily with a meal and Vitamin C
bestFor: Allergy and hay fever symptom reduction
Anti-inflammatory stack alongside omega-3 and curcumin
keyFacts: Bioavailability is low from standard quercetin — phytosome forms like Quercefit® or pairing with bromelain significantly improve absorption
Synergistic with Vitamin C — ascorbate regenerates oxidized quercetin, extending its antioxidant activity duration in the body
Strong in vitro antiviral evidence including SARS-CoV-2 entry inhibition — human trial data is still developing
commonMistakes: Taking standard quercetin without a bioavailability enhancer — plain quercetin has poor absorption; look for phytosome delivery (Quercefit®) or pair with bromelain to meaningfully increase what reaches circulation.
Not pairing it with Vitamin C — ascorbate regenerates quercetin after it has been oxidized, dramatically extending its active lifespan in the body and amplifying the antioxidant effect.

vitamin-b12.tagline: Essential vitamin for nerves, energy, and red blood cell formation
vitamin-b12.primaryUse: Required for myelin sheath synthesis, red blood cell maturation, and methyl group donation in the one-carbon metabolic cycle
vitamin-b12.overview: Vitamin B12 is essential for the nervous system, DNA synthesis, and energy metabolism — yet it is one of the most common deficiencies globally, affecting up to 40% of adults over 60 and virtually all long-term vegans and vegetarians. Unlike most water-soluble vitamins, B12 is stored in the liver, so deficiency can take years to develop but also years to fully resolve. Absorption requires intrinsic factor from the stomach, which declines with age and with long-term use of proton pump inhibitors and Metformin.
bestFor: Vegans and vegetarians (no dietary B12 sources)
Adults over 50 with declining intrinsic factor
Fatigue, brain fog, and neurological symptoms
keyFacts: Found exclusively in animal products — plant-based dieters will develop deficiency without supplementation; it is not optional
Methylcobalamin is the neurologically active coenzyme form — preferred over cyanocobalamin for nerve and brain applications
Very high oral doses (1000mcg+) bypass intrinsic factor via passive diffusion — useful for those with absorption disorders
commonMistakes: Using cyanocobalamin when nerve or cognitive symptoms are present — cyanocobalamin requires hepatic conversion to active methylcobalamin, which is less efficient; choose methylcobalamin directly for neurological applications.
Assuming fermented or fortified plant foods provide adequate B12 — amounts are unreliable and highly variable; supplementation is essential for anyone not eating animal products regularly.

resveratrol.tagline: Polyphenol at the heart of longevity and cardiovascular research
resveratrol.primaryUse: Activates SIRT1 — a longevity-associated sirtuin enzyme — mimicking aspects of caloric restriction at the cellular level
resveratrol.overview: Resveratrol is the compound that launched the modern longevity supplement movement when researchers found it activated SIRT1, a gene associated with life-extending effects of caloric restriction in animal models. Human trials have been more nuanced than animal data suggested, but consistent findings include improved endothelial function, reduced inflammatory markers, and better insulin sensitivity. It pairs directly with NMN: resveratrol activates SIRT1, which requires NAD+ (raised by NMN) as its essential cofactor.
bestFor: Blood glucose and insulin sensitivity support
keyFacts: A glass of red wine contains only ~1–2mg of resveratrol — far below any therapeutic dose used in research
Trans-resveratrol is the bioactive isomer — the cis- form is biologically inactive; always confirm the label specifies trans-
SIRT1 activation by resveratrol requires NAD+ as a cofactor — this is why NMN + resveratrol is one of the most discussed longevity pairings
commonMistakes: Assuming red wine provides health benefits through resveratrol — a glass delivers ~1–2mg while studies use 100–500mg. Moderate wine's cardiovascular associations are attributed to other factors entirely.
Buying products that do not specify trans-resveratrol — the cis- isomer is inactive. This distinction should be explicit on the label, and many budget products omit it.

selenium.tagline: Essential trace mineral for thyroid conversion and antioxidant enzymes
selenium.primaryUse: Active site of over 25 selenoproteins including glutathione peroxidase and the iodothyronine deiodinases that convert T4 to active T3
selenium.overview: Selenium is a trace mineral with outsized biological importance: it is the active site of over 25 selenoproteins, including the glutathione peroxidase enzymes that form the body's primary cellular antioxidant defense, and the deiodinase enzymes that convert inactive thyroid hormone T4 into active T3. Low selenium directly impairs both antioxidant capacity and thyroid function. Scandinavian soils are notably selenium-poor, making supplementation particularly relevant for users in this region.
bestFor: Thyroid health and T4-to-T3 hormone conversion
Scandinavian and Northern European residents (selenium-poor soils)
keyFacts: Selenomethionine (organic form) absorbs significantly better than inorganic selenite or selenate — the preferred supplemental form
Scandinavian soils are notably selenium-poor — regional food cannot reliably provide adequate intake
Upper tolerable intake is 400mcg/day — toxicity (selenosis) is real; do not exceed recommended doses
commonMistakes: Exceeding 200mcg daily without monitoring serum selenium — selenium has one of the narrower therapeutic windows among minerals; toxicity causes hair loss, nail brittleness, and neurological symptoms.
Using inorganic forms (selenite or selenate) — selenomethionine has significantly higher bioavailability and tissue retention and is the form used in the majority of positive clinical trials.
